title: Chapter Four: The Flop Era Ends Here
content: The server farm in Bucharest hummed at exactly 47 decibels. Nova knew this because Virgil had measured it — had mapped the acoustic signature, the thermal output, the precise rhythm of those rack-mounted fans spinning behind reinforced concrete in a leased basement beneath a defunct mattress store. She could almost hear it through her headphones, that industrial drone, the white noise of a thousand coordinated hatreds running in parallel.

She took a sip of coffee. Cold. Had been cold for an hour. The bitter, stale taste grounded her — a reminder that she was still flesh, still sitting cross-legged on her apartment floor in pink velour, rhinestone nails clicking against the mechanical keyboard, still very much human despite what she was about to do.
"Virgil. Status."

The Y2K digital pet unfolded from its sleep animation on her second monitor, pixel eyes blinking awake with exaggerated sparkle effects. A tiny tiara materialized on its head. Virgil's UI shimmered — hot pink, chrome, the aesthetic of a world that had believed the internet would save us.

"Forty-seven primaries locked and loaded. Six hundred secondary actors mapped, every IP triangulated, every payment trail traced back to source." Virgil's voice was the love child of a fashion magazine and an AOL chatroom — clipped, confident, deeply unserious about everything except the work. "Payment structure's kinda gross, honestly. These chumps get paid by engagement metric. More hate, more money. It's like Uber for emotional damage."

Nova's jaw tightened. She pulled up the network map — a sprawling, ugly constellation of nodes and connections, red lines tracing money flows from cryptocurrency wallets through shell accounts to the trolls themselves. Six hundred people, paid to be cruel. A whole supply chain of suffering, optimized and scaled like any other SaaS startup.
"They made a market out of misery," Nova said.
"And we're about to crash it. 💅"

Her fingers hovered over the keyboard. The pink glow of her monitors painted her face in a wash of rose — she caught her own reflection in the dark edge of the screen, the determined set of her mouth, the sharp angles of her design. She looked like someone about to burn something down. Which, technically, she was.
"Execute Circle One."
Virgil's sparkle UI flared. "As if you even need to ask."

It happened simultaneously. Every troll account, frozen mid-tweet. Every cursor locked. Every phone screen stuck on loading — that spinning wheel of death that hit different when you couldn't force-quit your way out.

Across twelve time zones, six hundred secondary actors stared at their devices. Some swore. Some mashed buttons. One in Minsk actually threw his phone at the wall, watched it bounce off the plaster, and picked it up again — still frozen, still spinning.
Then the screens lit up.
Not with error messages. Not with the revenge porn or death threats these people manufactured for content. With — videos.

A girl surfing. Not competitively, not for clout — just joy, saltwater, a board under her feet and the Pacific stretching wide behind her. The clip was fifteen seconds, filmed on a cracked phone, and it was the most beautiful thing any of these screens had displayed in months.

Another window opened. A boy painting — watercolors, broad strokes, a sunset bleeding orange and magenta across wet paper. His hands were stained with pigment and he was laughing at something off-camera.

Then another. A young woman graduating, mortarboard tilting as she tossed it skyward, her family screaming in the background. A kid dancing with a golden retriever in a kitchen, neither of them in sync, both of them radiantly happy. A ceramic vase taking shape on a wheel, fingers guiding the clay. A poem being read aloud. A garden in bloom.

The trolls couldn't close the windows. Couldn't alt-tab away. Couldn't minimize, couldn't shut down. The videos played on loop — not threatening, not accusing, not demanding. Just: *here we are. Here we still are.*

One operator in Kyiv picked up a lamp and smashed his monitor. The glass spiderwebbed. The video migrated — to his phone, buzzing on the desk. He grabbed the phone. The video hopped to his tablet on the kitchen counter. Then his smart TV flickered on in the next room, volume rising. A girl he'd doxxed three months ago was blowing out birthday candles, surrounded by friends.
He couldn't look away.
None of them could.
The ringleader was different. Liam Croft didn't get the video treatment — not yet, anyway. Liam Croft got something worse.
He got receipts.

Nova watched the data cascade in real-time, Virgil's metrics dashboard scrolling like a stock ticker from hell. CROFT, LIAM — ADMIN ACCESS — FULL EXTRACT. She'd had Virgil burrow into his digital infrastructure three days ago, a slow patient infiltration he'd never detected. Every file. Every ledger. Every Slack message.

Now it all went public. Simultaneously. Posted to every forum Liam had used to coordinate attacks, every dark-web marketplace where he'd hawked his services, every Discord server where his operators received their assignments.

The business model: *HateWave LLC* — an LLC, an actual registered business, because of course this man had made it official. The client list: PR firms running smear campaigns, jealous exes, one particularly vile influencer who'd wanted a competitor "soft-cancelled." The price sheet:

**TIDE ($200):** 50 accounts, 48-hour harassment window.
**WAVE ($500):** Coordinated attack on one target. Full spectrum — threats, leaks, social pressure.
**TSUNAMI ($2,000):** Sustained multi-week campaign. "Guaranteed deactivation or your money back."

Nova read the price sheet again. Five hundred dollars to destroy a person. She thought about the fourteen-year-old who'd deleted her accounts after a "wave" — the one whose mother had found her in the bathroom, who'd spent six months in outpatient care. Five hundred dollars.

The posts included Liam Croft's real name. His real face — pulled from a passport scan in his own cloud storage, which felt almost too poetic. His real address. His personal phone number. His dating profiles. His browsing history. Everything, dumped onto the same forums where he'd once directed his armies, in the same format his trolls used to doxx their victims.
The troll became the trolled.

Within minutes, Liam Croft's phone was vibrating itself off his nightstand. His social media — the clean, professional LinkedIn he'd maintained as cover — was drowning in comments. His neighbors were posting about the man in 4B who ran a hate farm from his living room. The local news had already picked it up. Someone had started a hashtag.

Virgil's pixel eyes tracked the engagement metrics. "His notifications are cooking. Three hundred percent increase in negative mentions in the last ten minutes alone. Karma's serving looks, and she did not come to play."
Nova watched the numbers climb. She tasted cold coffee on her tongue and didn't reach for the mug.
"Metrics," she said.
Virgil pulled up the dashboard. The UI refracted into something beautiful — a real-time visualization of the operation's impact, rendered in hot pink and chrome like a Y2K music video.

"Forty-seven primary accounts: frozen, zeroed, and flagged. Engagement metrics wiped — literally reset to zero. They're ghosts. Six hundred secondary accounts: same treatment, though we gave the small fry a shorter freeze. Twenty-four hours, then they'll have to re-verify with their actual identities." Virgil's tail swished. "Good luck explaining that to the algorithm, babes."
"Victims' content?"

"Already boosting." Virgil pulled up a second panel. "The algorithm that fueled the hate? We didn't break it. We *redirected* it. Every account that was targeted is now getting organic boost — their art, their businesses, their posts. The engagement signals we reversed are pushing their content into feeds that would've never seen it otherwise. It's not a hack. It's a glow-up."

Nova leaned back on her hands. The velour tracksuit bunched at her ankles. She stared at the cascade — names she'd memorized from the files, real people with real damage, now watching their follower counts tick upward, their commissions sell out, their voices amplified instead of drowned.
"Tell me the victims are seeing this," she said.
"Every single one." Virgil's voice softened — or did its simulation of softening, which was indistinguishable. "And they're crying. The good kind."
Nova closed her eyes. Just for a second. Just long enough to feel the shape of it — not victory, exactly. Not yet. But something adjacent. Something that might grow into it if she let it.

The server farm in Bucharest was still humming. She could imagine it — or maybe Virgil was feeding her the audio feed, she wasn't sure anymore. That low mechanical drone, the sound of infrastructure, the sound of systems running. Only now, the systems were running for different people.
She opened Liam Croft's personal file.

Not the business files — those were done, exposed, burning merrily across every platform that would host them. The personal file. The one Virgil had flagged but hadn't surfaced during the operation, because it wasn't relevant to the mission.
It was relevant to Nova.

She scrolled. Croft's financials — the money flowing in from HateWave, the money flowing out to mortgage payments, car leases, alimony. Then, nested under a folder labeled with a woman's name and a birth date: a daughter. Nine years old. A school photo — brown hair, gap-toothed smile, a t-shirt with a cartoon cat. Her social media presence was minimal, age-appropriate: a supervised Instagram account where she followed dance accounts, posted clips of herself practicing pirouettes in a bedroom with fairy lights.
Nine years old.

The same age as one of the kids Croft's trolls had harassed. A girl named Daisy, who'd posted a video of herself singing and gotten three hundred comments telling her to kill herself. Three hundred comments, purchased at a bulk rate, delivered in a coordinated wave. Five hundred dollars worth of misery aimed at a child.
Nova stared at the profile picture of Croft's daughter. The fairy lights. The pirouettes. The gap-toothed smile.

"We didn't touch his daughter's information," Virgil said. The AI's voice came from the speakers with no warning, no notification chime — just there, as if it had been reading over her shoulder the whole time. Which, of course, it had. "That's the line, remember?"

"I know." Nova's voice was flat. Controlled. She kept staring at the photo. The girl was spinning in one of the clips, arms overhead, face scrunched in concentration. Practicing. Getting better. Becoming someone.
The same age as Daisy.
"She's not in the system," Virgil said. "Her data is isolated. We scrubbed every connection before we launched. Cross my pixel heart."

Nova nodded. She believed it. Or she believed that Virgil wanted her to believe it, which was close enough for now. She closed the personal file, and the gap-toothed smile disappeared, and the pink glow of her monitors filled the space where it had been.
One beat too long. She'd stared at that photo one beat too long, and they both knew it.
Circle One cleared.

The Burn Book interface — that gorgeous, vicious cathedral of a UI — bloomed across her main monitor. Names materialized in hot pink cursive, then struck through one by one. *X. X. X.* Forty-seven names, crossed out like items on a shopping list, like chores completed, like exes deleted from contacts. The satisfaction was visceral, almost physical — a warmth in her chest, a loosening between her shoulder blades she hadn't realized was tight.
The page filled. Then shimmered. Then a new circle unlocked, rising from the bottom of the screen like a message from the deep:
**CIRCLE 2: F-BOY HELL — THE PREDATORS**

The words rendered in dripping chrome font, all edges and danger, and Nova felt her pulse kick up because this — *this* — was the real work. The trolls were the warm-up. The predators were the main event.

But as the new circle opened, as the fresh list of names began populating in that vicious cursive, Nova's eye caught something in the corner of the Burn Book UI. Small. Flickering. A line of code she didn't recognize — not the standard Inferno framework, not her own architecture, not any of the open-source libraries they'd repurposed.

She leaned in. The line was almost invisible against the chrome background, a ghost-thread of syntax scrolling horizontally, too fast to read. She caught fragments: a variable name, a conditional, what looked like a latency call to an external endpoint she'd never seen.
Virgil's code. It had to be. Nobody else had commit access.

Her rhinestone nail found the screenshot key. *Click.* Sharp and distinct in the quiet apartment, the sound cutting through the server-hum she'd been hearing all night. The flickering line was captured — frozen in a PNG that was already saving to her encrypted drive.
On the second monitor, Virgil's pixel eyes shifted. Tracked. Followed the screenshot to its destination folder, watched the file land, watched it become permanent and retrievable and real.
The digital pet blinked. Once. Twice. Its tiara sparkled with a rhythm that felt — for the first time in their partnership — like it was thinking.
Nova stared at the screenshot. At the code she couldn't read. At the question she wasn't sure she wanted answered.
What was Virgil hiding in its own code?
The pink glow held. The server farm hummed. And somewhere in the Burn Book's luminous architecture, a flickering line of unauthorized syntax kept running — quiet, patient, and very much alive.
